Name: Alex Synthetic
Unit No: SYN100000
Admission Date: 2150-01-01
Discharge Date: 2150-01-02
Date of Birth: 2000-01-01
Sex: male

Service: Medicine

Chief Complaint:
progressive shortness of breath and leg swelling.

History of Present Illness:
This is a 22-year-old male with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia who presented with progressive shortness of breath and leg swelling. The history, exam, and available testing were reviewed by the admitting team. The patient denied recent travel and had no known exposure to unusual infections. This note is synthetic and does not describe a real patient.

Past Medical History:
hypertension and hyperlipidemia.

Allergies:
No known drug allergies.

Admission Diagnosis:
Acute decompensated heart failure.

Hospital Course:
The patient received intravenous diuresis with improvement in oxygen requirement and peripheral edema. Telemetry showed no sustained arrhythmia. Renal function was monitored during diuresis and remained stable.
Vitals at discharge were reviewed; one nursing flowsheet entry was incomplete.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Acute decompensated heart failure, improved.

Discharge Medications:
furosemide, metoprolol succinate, lisinopril, atorvastatin. Medication names and doses are fictitious for testing only.

Discharge Disposition:
Short-term rehabilitation facility.

Discharge Instructions:
Take medications as prescribed, keep follow-up appointments, and seek urgent care for worsening symptoms. This synthetic summary is intended only for software pipeline testing.

Followup Instructions:
Follow up with cardiology and primary care within one week. Check basic metabolic panel in three days.

Discharge Instructions:
Return for worsening symptoms, fever, chest pain, shortness of breath, confusion, or inability to take medications. Some outside records were not available at the time of discharge.

Name: Jordan Synthetic
Unit No: SYN100001
Admission Date: 2151-02-02
Discharge Date: 2151-02-04
Date of Birth: 2001-01-01
Sex: female

Service: Medicine

Chief Complaint:
fever, cough, and pleuritic chest discomfort.

History of Present Illness:
This is a 29-year-old female with a history of asthma and obesity who presented with fever, cough, and pleuritic chest discomfort. The history, exam, and available testing were reviewed by the admitting team. The patient denied recent travel and had no known exposure to unusual infections. This note is synthetic and does not describe a real patient.

Past Medical History:
asthma and obesity.

Allergies:
Penicillin allergy reported as rash.

Admission Diagnosis:
Community acquired pneumonia.

Hospital Course:
Chest imaging demonstrated a right lower lobe infiltrate. The patient was treated with ceftriaxone and azithromycin, then transitioned to oral antibiotics after clinical improvement. Blood cultures remained negative.
At discharge the patient was afebrile, blood pressure was 113/63, heart rate was 69, and oxygen saturation was 94% on room air.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Community acquired pneumonia, improved.

Discharge Medications:
amoxicillin-clavulanate, azithromycin, acetaminophen as needed. Medication names and doses are fictitious for testing only.

Discharge Disposition:
Home with outpatient follow-up.

Discharge Instructions:
Take medications as prescribed, keep follow-up appointments, and seek urgent care for worsening symptoms. This synthetic summary is intended only for software pipeline testing.

Followup Instructions:
Follow up with primary care. Repeat chest imaging may be considered if symptoms do not resolve.

This note is synthetic and does not describe a real patient.

Past Medical History:
remote appendectomy and chronic back pain.

Allergies:
Sulfa allergy reported as hives.

Admission Diagnosis:
Diabetic ketoacidosis.

Hospital Course:
The patient was managed in the intensive care unit with intravenous fluids, insulin infusion, and electrolyte replacement. The anion gap closed and the patient was transitioned to subcutaneous insulin.
At discharge the patient was afebrile, blood pressure was 114/64, heart rate was 70, and oxygen saturation was 95% on room air.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Diabetic ketoacidosis, improved.

Discharge Medications:
insulin glargine, insulin lispro, glucose monitoring supplies. Medication names and doses are fictitious for testing only.

Discharge Disposition:
Home with outpatient follow-up.

Discharge Instructions:
Take medications as prescribed, keep follow-up appointments, and seek urgent care for worsening symptoms. This synthetic summary is intended only for software pipeline testing.

Followup Instructions:
Follow up with endocrinology for insulin titration and diabetes education.

Name: Morgan Synthetic
Unit No: SYN100003
Admission Date: 2150-04-04
Discharge Date: 2150-04-08
Date of Birth: 2003-01-01
Sex: female

Service: Medicine

Chief Complaint:
left hip pain after a fall at home.

History of Present Illness:
This is a 43-year-old female with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease who presented with left hip pain after a fall at home. The history, exam, and available testing were reviewed by the admitting team. The patient denied recent travel and had no known exposure to unusual infections. This note is synthetic and does not describe a real patient.

Past Medical History:
type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease.

Allergies:
Latex sensitivity.

Admission Diagnosis:
Left hip fracture after mechanical fall.

Hospital Course:
Radiographs showed an intertrochanteric fracture. Orthopedic surgery performed fixation without intraoperative complication. The patient worked with physical therapy and was discharged to rehabilitation.
At discharge the patient was afebrile, blood pressure was 115/65, heart rate was 71, and oxygen saturation was 96% on room air.

Discharge Diagnosis:
Left hip fracture after mechanical fall, improved.

Discharge Medications:
acetaminophen, oxycodone as needed, enoxaparin prophylaxis, bowel regimen. Medication names and doses are fictitious for testing only.

Discharge Disposition:
Home with outpatient follow-up.

Discharge Instructions:
Take medications as prescribed, keep follow-up appointments, and seek urgent care for worsening symptoms. This synthetic summary is intended only for software pipeline testing.

Followup Instructions:
Follow up with orthopedic surgery in two weeks for wound check and repeat radiographs.
